---
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-level: 2
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "kerasnip v0.1.0.900"
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
execute:
  freeze: auto
  cache: true
---

```{r}
#| label: madrid-2-2026-cache-results
#| include: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Compute results once, cache them on disk, and reuse across EN/ES renders.
#
# Quarto executes this document with working directory = `presentations/madrid-2-2026/`,
# so cache files should live in `presentations/madrid-2-2026/results/` (i.e. `results/` here).
results_dir <- "results"
old_results_dirs <- c(
  file.path("..", "..", "madrid-2026", "results"),
  file.path("madrid-2026", "results")
)
dir.create(results_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

# Migration:
# - Before the folder move, cached results lived at `madrid-2026/results/`.
# - Older versions could also write nested `madrid-2026/results/` inside the project dir.
for (old_results_dir in old_results_dirs) {
  if (!dir.exists(old_results_dir)) {
    next
  }

  old_rds <- list.files(old_results_dir, pattern = "\\.rds$", full.names = TRUE)
  if (length(old_rds) > 0) {
    file.copy(old_rds, results_dir, overwrite = FALSE)
  }
}

diamonds_rds <- file.path(results_dir, "diamonds_res.rds")
diamonds_final_rds <- file.path(results_dir, "diamonds_final.rds")
attrition_rds <- file.path(results_dir, "attrition_res.rds")
attrition_final_rds <- file.path(results_dir, "attrition_final.rds")

set.seed(20260203)

can_run <- function(...) {
  all(vapply(list(...), requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE))
}

if (can_run("tidymodels", "kerasnip", "keras3")) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(tidymodels)
    library(kerasnip)
    library(keras3)
  })

  data(diamonds, package = "ggplot2")

  input_block <- function(model, input_shape) {
    keras_model_sequential(input_shape = input_shape)
  }

  dense_block <- function(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.0) {
    model |>
      layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
      layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
  }

  output_block <- function(model) {
    model |> layer_dense(units = 1)
  }

  create_keras_sequential_spec(
    model_name = "diamond_mlp",
    layer_blocks = list(
      input = input_block,
      body = dense_block,
      output = output_block
    ),
    mode = "regression"
  )

  diamonds_split <- initial_split(diamonds, strata = price)
  diamonds_train <- training(diamonds_split)

  diamonds_rec <- recipe(price ~ ., data = diamonds_train) |>
    step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
    step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
    step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

  spec <- diamond_mlp(
    num_body = tune(),
    body_units = tune(),
    body_dropout = tune(),
    fit_epochs = 5
  ) |>
    set_engine("keras")

  wf <- workflow() |>
    add_recipe(diamonds_rec) |>
    add_model(spec)

  params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>
    update(
      num_body = dials::num_terms(c(1, 3)),
      body_units = dials::hidden_units(c(32, 128)),
      body_dropout = dials::dropout(c(0, 0.3))
    )

  grid <- grid_latin_hypercube(params, size = 8)
  folds <- vfold_cv(diamonds_train, v = 3, strata = price)

  if (!file.exists(diamonds_rds)) {
    res <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid, control = control_grid())
    saveRDS(res, diamonds_rds)
  } else {
    res <- readRDS(diamonds_rds)
  }

  if (!file.exists(diamonds_final_rds)) {
    best <- select_best(res, metric = "rmse")
    final_wf <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)
    lf <- last_fit(final_wf, split = diamonds_split, metrics = metric_set(rmse, mae, rsq))

    diamonds_final <- list(
      best = best,
      metrics = collect_metrics(lf),
      preds = collect_predictions(lf)
    )
    saveRDS(diamonds_final, diamonds_final_rds)
  }
}

if (can_run("tidymodels", "modeldata", "kerasnip", "keras3")) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(tidymodels)
    library(modeldata)
    library(kerasnip)
    library(keras3)
  })

  data(attrition)

  # Correct functional signatures per kerasnip docs:
  input_block <- function(input_shape) {
    layer_input(shape = input_shape, name = "features")
  }

  dense_block <- function(tensor, units = 64, dropout = 0.0) {
    tensor |>
      layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
      layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
  }

  concat_block <- function(input_a, input_b) {
    layer_concatenate(list(input_a, input_b))
  }

  output_block <- function(tensor, num_classes) {
    tensor |> layer_dense(units = num_classes, activation = "softmax")
  }

  create_keras_functional_spec(
    model_name = "attrition_towers",
    layer_blocks = list(
      main_input = input_block,
      tower_a = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
      tower_b = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
      joined = inp_spec(concat_block, c(input_a = "tower_a", input_b = "tower_b")),
      output = inp_spec(output_block, "joined")
    ),
    mode = "classification"
  )

  split <- initial_split(attrition, strata = Attrition)
  train <- training(split)

  rec <- recipe(Attrition ~ ., data = train) |>
    step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
    step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
    step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

  spec <- attrition_towers(
    tower_a_units = tune(),
    tower_b_units = tune(),
    fit_epochs = 5
  ) |>
    set_engine("keras")

  wf <- workflow() |>
    add_recipe(rec) |>
    add_model(spec)

  params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>
    update(
      tower_a_units = dials::hidden_units(c(16, 128)),
      tower_b_units = dials::hidden_units(c(16, 128))
    )

  grid <- grid_latin_hypercube(params, size = 8)
  folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 3, strata = Attrition)

  if (!file.exists(attrition_rds)) {
    res <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid, metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy))
    saveRDS(res, attrition_rds)
  } else {
    res <- readRDS(attrition_rds)
  }

  if (!file.exists(attrition_final_rds)) {
    best <- select_best(res, metric = "roc_auc")
    final_wf <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)
    lf <- last_fit(final_wf, split = split, metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy))

    preds <- collect_predictions(lf)
    event_level <- levels(preds$Attrition)[1]
    preferred <- paste0(".pred_", event_level)
    pos_col <- if (preferred %in% names(preds)) {
      preferred
    } else {
      pcols <- grep("^\\.pred_", names(preds), value = TRUE)
      pcols <- setdiff(pcols, ".pred_class")
      pcols[1]
    }

    attrition_final <- list(
      best = best,
      metrics = collect_metrics(lf),
      preds = preds,
      roc = if (!is.na(pos_col)) {
        roc_curve(preds, truth = Attrition, !!rlang::sym(pos_col))
      } else {
        NULL
      }
    )
    saveRDS(attrition_final, attrition_final_rds)
  }
}
```

::: {.content-visible when-profile="english"}

## kerasnip: A Bridge Between 'keras' and 'tidymodels' {.title-slide background-color="#f0f1f2"}

### Madrid R Group | February 2026
**kerasnip v0.1.0.900**

Docs: [davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip](https://davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip/) Â· Repo: [github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip](https://github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip)

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Hook the audience with the core conflict.

**Stage Directions**:
- Start energetic.
- Ask: "How many of you love `tidymodels`?" (Wait for hands).
- Ask: "How many of you fight with Keras architectures?"

**The Hook**:
- Keras and Tidymodels are like **Oil and Water**.
- **Keras** is an imperative builder (you write code to *do* things).
- **Tidymodels** is a declarative configuration (you describe *what* you want).
- `kerasnip` is the emulsifier that lets them mix.

**Key Takeaway**: Today is about solving the *architectural tuning* problem.
:::

---

## Today

- Why tuning Keras inside tidymodels is hard
- What `kerasnip` generates (and why it matters)
- Sequential workflow: structural tuning with `num_{block}`
- Functional workflow: branch/merge graphs with `inp_spec()`
- Debugging + practical tips

::: {.notes}
Goal: orient people so they donâ€™t get lost later.

Say:
- Timing is flexible; clarity first.
- First half: sequential workflow + structural tuning (`num_{block}`) + debugging.
- Second half: functional graphs via `inp_spec()` with the same tuning workflow.

Also mention (fast):
- Debug helpers: `extract_valid_grid()`, `inform_errors()`.
- Keras object helpers: `keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`, `optimizer_function()`, `loss_function_keras()`.
- Spec lifecycle: `model_exists()`, `remove_keras_spec()`, `get_model_env()`.

Transition: Letâ€™s situate Keras vs tidymodels and name the gap.
:::

---

## The Landscape

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### đź§± Keras (via `keras3`)
- **Deep Learning**
- Tensor-centric & Imperative
- Backend agnostic (TF, JAX, Torch)
- Arbitrary graphs of layers
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### đź§ą Tidymodels
- **Machine Learning**
- Dataframe-centric & Declarative
- Unified interface (`fit`, `predict`)
- Standardized tuning
:::
:::

**The Gap**: Using `tune_grid()` to optimize Neural Network topology (depth/width) is difficult because `parsnip` expects fixed arguments, while Keras models are arbitrary graphs.

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Anchor the mental model using the "Architect vs. Form Filler" analogy.

**Analogy**:
- **Keras** is an **Architect's Table**. You have a blank sheet, rulers, and pencils. You can draw *anything* (a house, a skyscraper, a bridge).
- **Tidymodels** is a **Government Form**. It asks specific questions: "Number of floors?", "Color of walls?". It's easy to process (tune), but you can't "fill in a form" to invent a new kind of building.

**The Solution**:
- We need a way to turn the *structure of the building* into fields on the form.
- That's what `kerasnip` does: it lets you define the blueprint, and then exposes the "knobs" (depth, width) as standard arguments.
:::

---

## The Pain Point (in one sentence)

Keras models are **arbitrary graphs**, but `parsnip` specs are **fixed argument sets**.

So you can tune learning rate easily, but tuning *model structure* (depth / width / branches) is awkward.

::: {.notes}
Goal: make the problem feel concrete and familiar.

Say:
- This is not â€śdeep learning 101â€ť. Itâ€™s: how to keep Keras *inside* the tidymodels workflow.
- The friction is that architecture changes live in Keras code, but tidymodels tuning expects named arguments.

Transition: So the question becomes: how do we turn topology decisions into tunable spec args?
:::

---

## Ecosystem Context

| Package | Role | Limitation |
|---------|------|------------|
| `parsnip::mlp()` | Tidymodels MLP | Mostly fixed topology; little architecture/feature engineering |
| `reticulate` | Python Bridge | Low-level, no ML workflow |
| `keras3` | R Interface | No native `tune()` integration |
| `luz`/`brulee` | Torch + Tidymodels | Torch-only backend |
| **`kerasnip`** | **Keras + Tidymodels** | **Bridges Keras flexibility with Tidymodels workflows** |

::: {.notes}
Goal: position kerasnip precisely (avoid confusion).

Say:
- Not replacing `keras3`.
- Not a new engine.
- It generates *new parsnip model specs* that still use the existing `keras` engine.

Also say explicitly:
- `parsnip::mlp()` is great for standard MLPs, but limited for architecture search.
- kerasnip focuses on tuning *structure* (depth/width/branches) without leaving tidymodels.

Transition: Now: what â€śdynamic specsâ€ť means in practice.
:::

---

## The Solution: Dynamic Specs

`kerasnip` is a **Model Builder**, not a Model Zoo.

1.  **Define Blocks**: Functions returning Keras layers.
2.  **Meta-program Spec**: `create_keras_*_spec()` generates a new `parsnip` model.
3.  **Tune Structure**: Block arguments (and repetition count) can be tuned.

::: {.notes}
Goal: explain the â€śdynamic specâ€ť idea in one breath.

Say:
- This is a code-generation approach: you define blocks, then `kerasnip` generates a parsnip spec function.
- Once itâ€™s a parsnip spec, the rest is standard: recipes â†’ workflows â†’ `tune_grid()`.

Transition: Keep the mental model in mind; weâ€™ll come back to it on every example.
:::

---

## Mental Model {.mermaid-wide}

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
 
flowchart LR
  classDef step fill:#f5f7fa,stroke:#5c6b7a,color:#1b3a57,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef tune fill:#BF281B,stroke:#BF281B,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;

  A["Blocks<br/>(layer blocks)"] --> B["Spec function<br/>create_keras_*_spec()"]
  B --> C["Workflow<br/>recipe() + workflow()"]
  C --> D["Tuning<br/>tune_grid()"]
  A -. "args â†’ tunables<br/>num_{block} repeats" .-> B

  class A,B,C step;
  class D tune;
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: anchor the whole talk to one repeatable mental model.

Say:
- If someone gets lost: blocks â†’ spec â†’ workflow â†’ tuning.

Under the hood (if asked):
- Specs are registered in an internal environment.
- Thatâ€™s why there are lifecycle helpers: `model_exists()`, `get_model_env()`, `remove_keras_spec()`.

Transition: Next: the minimal set of functions youâ€™ll see in the examples.
:::

---

## Key Functions Youâ€™ll See

- `create_keras_sequential_spec()`
- `create_keras_functional_spec()`
- `inp_spec()` (functional wiring)
- `compile_keras_grid()` (debug/inspect generated graphs)
- `extract_parameter_set_dials()` (what can be tuned?)

::: {.notes}
Goal: de-risk the API surface area.

Say:
- No need to memorize; just map each function to its role.

Bonus: other kerasnip functions you may want later (not all shown in code today):
- Debugging the compilation stage: `extract_valid_grid()` (keep only successful builds), `inform_errors()` (summarize common compile failures).
- Evaluation + introspection: `extract_keras_model()`, `extract_keras_history()`, `keras_evaluate()`.
- Keras object discovery + tuning: `keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`, `optimizer_function()`, `loss_function_keras()`.
- Housekeeping: `model_exists()`, `remove_keras_spec()`, `get_model_env()`.

Transition: Next: naming conventions, so the code reads naturally.
:::

---

## Naming Conventions (auto-generated args)

- Arguments get **prefixed** by block name (e.g. `body_units`)
- Repeatable blocks expose `num_{block}` (e.g. `num_body`)
- Repetitions **share** the same hyperparameters (by design)

Example mapping:

`dense_block(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.1)` â†’ `body_units`, `body_dropout`

::: {.notes}
Goal: remove naming confusion before we touch code.

Say:
- Block args become spec args with prefixes (e.g. `body_units`).
- Repetition is controlled by `num_{block}`.
- Repeated blocks share hyperparameters by design.

Transition: With that mapping, the sequential example will look â€śnormalâ€ť.
:::

---

## Sequential vs Functional (rule of thumb)

- **Sequential**: one stream of features, simple stacks
- **Functional**: multi-input, branch/merge, skip connections, explicit graphs

::: {.notes}
Goal: give a decision rule people can remember.

Say:
- Use sequential for â€śa chain of layersâ€ť.
- Use functional when you need an explicit graph: branches/merges, skips, multi-input.

Transition: Weâ€™ll start sequential (easier), then come back to functional.
:::

---

## Common Gotchas

- Ensure the recipe outputs numeric matrices (dummy + normalize)
- Start with a small grid, confirm structure via `compile_keras_grid()`
- Keep tuning spaces reasonable (depth Ă— width can explode)

::: {.notes}
Goal: preempt the top failure modes.

Say:
- This slide is here to save pain during Q&A.

Extra gotchas to mention out loud:
- Classification: make sure the outcome is a factor (tidymodels convention) and the final layer/loss matches binary vs multiclass.
- Every grid point is a *fresh model build + fresh training*; set seeds if you want stable comparisons.
- Training controls (epochs/callbacks) should be held constant during architecture search unless you intentionally tune them.

Transition: With those guardrails, letâ€™s run a simple sequential example.
:::

---

## Workflow 1: Sequential API (Regression)

**Goal**: Predict diamond prices and tune **depth + width**.

Dataset: `ggplot2::diamonds`.

::: {.notes}
Goal: set up a simple, credible demo.

Say:
- This is a tabular MLP on diamonds. Simple on purpose: the point is tuning depth/width, not feature engineering.

Transition: First we define blocks (just functions), then generate the spec.
:::

---

## Sequential Blocks (minimal)

```r
input_block <- function(model, input_shape) {
  keras_model_sequential(input_shape = input_shape)
}

dense_block <- function(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.0) {
  model |>
    layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
    layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
}

output_block <- function(model) {
  model |> layer_dense(units = 1)
}
```

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Show that "Blocks" are just standard R functions.

**Walkthrough**:
- Point to `input_block`: "It just returns a model. No magic yet."
- Point to `dense_block`: "This looks exactly like standard Keras code, right?"
- **The Trick**: The function signature `(model, units, dropout)` defines what will be tunable later.
- If you want to tune it, make it an argument.

**Best Practice**:
- Keep blocks "pure" and deterministic.
- Repetitions (via `num_body`) share these hyperparameters by design.
:::

---

## Generate the `parsnip` spec

```r
create_keras_sequential_spec(
  model_name = "diamond_mlp",
  layer_blocks = list(
    input = input_block,
    body = dense_block,  # <--- This block will be repeatable
    output = output_block
  ),
  mode = "regression"
)
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: make the â€śspec generationâ€ť moment land.

Say:
- This creates a new parsnip spec function: `diamond_mlp()`.
- From this point on, it behaves like any other tidymodels model: you can set engine, add to workflows, and tune args.

Transition: Next weâ€™ll tune topology via `num_body`.
:::

---

## Structural Tuning

We treat **topology** as a hyperparameter.

```r
spec <- diamond_mlp(
  num_body = tune(),       # <--- Depth (e.g., 1, 2, or 3 layers)
  body_units = tune(),     # <--- Width (e.g., 64 vs 128 units)
  body_dropout = tune(),
  fit_epochs = 20
) |>
  set_engine("keras")
```

The arguments `num_{block}` are automatically generated by `kerasnip` for any repeatable block.

::: {.notes}
Goal: make â€śdepth is tunableâ€ť feel obvious.

Say:
- `num_body` allows searching topology inside a standard `tune_grid()`.

What to emphasize:
- Depth becomes a first-class parameter.
- Keep training controls mostly fixed during architecture search.

Practical tip:
- Tune structure first (depth/width), then optionally tune optimizer/training hyperparameters.

Transition: Next: put it into a normal workflow.
:::

---

## Diamonds + Tidymodels Workflow

```r
library(tidymodels)
library(kerasnip)

data(diamonds, package = "ggplot2")

diamonds_split <- initial_split(diamonds, strata = price)
diamonds_train <- training(diamonds_split)

diamonds_rec <- recipe(price ~ ., data = diamonds_train) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(diamonds_rec) |>
  add_model(spec)
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: show the tidymodels parity story.

Say:
- The workflow looks normal.
- The Keras complexity is â€śbehindâ€ť the parsnip spec.

If you want one extra credibility sentence:
- kerasnip handles the recipe â†’ Keras conversions via internal helpers like `process_x_sequential()` / `process_y_sequential()` and `generic_sequential_fit()`.

Transition: Next: define the tuning ranges and run `tune_grid()`.
:::

---

## Tuning Grid (including depth) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>
  update(
    num_body     = dials::num_terms(c(1, 4)),
    body_units   = dials::hidden_units(c(32, 256)),
    body_dropout = dials::dropout(c(0, 0.4))
  )

grid  <- grid_latin_hypercube(params, size = 20)
folds <- vfold_cv(diamonds_train, v = 5, strata = price)
res   <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid)
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: show this is just standard tidymodels tuning.

Say:
- `extract_parameter_set_dials()` discovers tunables, then we define ranges and build a grid.
- The key is that `num_body` is now a first-class tuning parameter.

Transition: Now letâ€™s look at CV results and pick a candidate before touching the test set.
:::

---

## Results (Diamonds): CV â€” Table

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-cv-table
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_res.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(tidymodels)
  })

  res <- readRDS(diamonds_rds)

  best_tbl <- show_best(res, metric = "rmse", n = 5)
  best_tbl <- best_tbl |>
    dplyr::select(num_body, body_units, body_dropout, mean, std_err) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3)))
  cat(knitr::kable(best_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("No cached results found yet. Render once to compute them.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: pick 1â€“2 strong candidates (donâ€™t read the whole table).

Say:
- These are cross-validation estimates (mean + std_err). Weâ€™re selecting on CV, not the test set.
- I usually look for a â€śstable regionâ€ť: low mean RMSE and not wildly sensitive to depth/dropout.

Transition: Letâ€™s look at the CV plot to see interactions.
:::

---

## Results (Diamonds): CV â€” Plot

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-cv-plot
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_res.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    library(tidymodels)
  })

  res <- readRDS(diamonds_rds)

  p_trends <- collect_metrics(res) |>
    filter(.metric == "rmse") |>
    ggplot(aes(x = num_body, y = mean, color = factor(body_units), group = interaction(body_units, body_dropout))) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_line(alpha = 0.35) +
    facet_wrap(~ body_dropout) +
    labs(
      x = "num_body (depth)",
      y = "CV RMSE (mean)",
      color = "body_units",
      title = "Cross-validation trends across depth/width"
    )
  print(p_trends)
} else {
  cat("No cached results found yet. Render once to compute them.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: see interactions (depth Ă— width Ă— dropout) at a glance.

Say:
- If the trend flattens, deeper models arenâ€™t buying much.
- When differences are tiny relative to noise, pick simpler.

Transition: Now we do a one-time test-set confirmation.
:::

---

## Results (Diamonds): Test â€” Table

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-test-table
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_final_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_final.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
  })

  final <- readRDS(diamonds_final_rds)

  metrics_tbl <- final$metrics |>
    dplyr::select(.metric, .estimate) |>
    dplyr::mutate(.estimate = round(.estimate, 4))
  cat(knitr::kable(metrics_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("No cached final results found yet.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: one-time confirmation.

Say:
- Test metrics are for final reporting; avoid using them to decide hyperparameters.
- If test is worse than CV by a lot, itâ€™s a sign of instability or leakage.

Transition: Iâ€™ll show a quick visual sanity check on test predictions.
:::

---

## Results (Diamonds): Test â€” Plot

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-test-plot
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_final_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_final.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(ggplot2)
  })

  final <- readRDS(diamonds_final_rds)

  p_test <- final$preds |>
    ggplot(aes(x = price, y = .pred)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linewidth = 0.8) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = "Truth (price)", y = "Predicted", title = "Final model on test set")
  print(p_test)
} else {
  cat("No cached final results found yet.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: keep slides uncluttered and interpret the final model.

Say:
- Show results only; talk over them.

Reproducibility workflow:

- Quarto `freeze: auto` runs once and reuses cached results.
- Manual: `Rscript presentations/madrid-2-2026/make_results.R` creates the same `.rds` artifacts.

Typical inspection steps:

`collect_metrics(res)` â†’ `show_best()` â†’ `select_best()` â†’ `finalize_workflow()` â†’ `last_fit()`

One more practical note:
- If you want to inspect the final Keras model, use `extract_keras_model()` and `extract_keras_history()` on the fitted workflow/fit object.

Transition: Next: how to debug/inspect the generated architecture.
:::

---

## Debugging: â€śWhat model did we just create?â€ť {.compact .table-compact}

```r
# compile_keras_grid(spec, grid, x, y)
```

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-debug
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tibble)
  library(purrr)
  library(tidymodels)
  library(kerasnip)
  library(keras3)
})

if (!exists("diamond_mlp", mode = "function") || !kerasnip::model_exists("diamond_mlp")) {
  input_block <- function(model, input_shape) {
    keras_model_sequential(input_shape = input_shape)
  }
  dense_block <- function(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.0) {
    model |>
      layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
      layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
  }
  output_block <- function(model) {
    model |> layer_dense(units = 1)
  }

  create_keras_sequential_spec(
    model_name = "diamond_mlp",
    layer_blocks = list(input = input_block, body = dense_block, output = output_block),
    mode = "regression"
  )
}

prep_rec <- prep(diamonds_rec, training = diamonds_train)
juiced <- juice(prep_rec)
x <- juiced |> select(-price)
y <- juiced$price

dbg <- compile_keras_grid(
  spec = diamond_mlp(
    num_body = tune(),
    body_units = tune(),
    body_dropout = tune(),
    fit_epochs = 5
  ) |> set_engine("keras"),
  grid = tibble(num_body = 3, body_units = 128, body_dropout = 0.2),
  x = x,
  y = y
)

dbg |> select(num_body, body_units, body_dropout, error)

if (!is.null(dbg$compiled_model[[1]])) {
  model <- dbg$compiled_model[[1]]
  tibble(
    n_features = ncol(x),
    n_layers = length(model$layers),
    n_params = model$count_params()
  )

  layer_tbl <- tibble(
    layer = map_chr(model$layers, "name"),
    class = map_chr(model$layers, ~ class(.x)[1])
  ) |>
    slice_head(n = 8)
  layer_tbl
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: keep sanity during structural search.

Say:
- Confirm depth/width changes are really happening.

Two extra helpers worth calling out:
- `extract_valid_grid(dbg)` filters to the grid points that compiled successfully.
- `inform_errors(dbg)` summarizes the most common compilation errors.

Cleanup tip:
- Use `remove_keras_spec("diamond_mlp")` when iterating interactively so you donâ€™t leave lots of generated specs around.

Transition: Next: switch to a functional (graph) example.
:::

---

## Workflow 2: Functional API (Branch/Merge)

**Goal**: Employee Attrition (Classification).

Dataset: `modeldata::attrition`.

Weâ€™ll build a small **branch/merge** graph:
- one input tensor
- two parallel â€śtowersâ€ť
- concat + output

::: {.notes}
Goal: motivate why functional Keras matters.

Say:
- Functional is the sweet spot when the architecture is not a straight line.

Important practical choice for a live talk: keep preprocessing simple.
We use ONE input produced by a standard recipe, then create two parallel branches from the same input.

Transition: `inp_spec()` is the wiring mechanism.
:::

---

## Functional Wiring with `inp_spec()`

`inp_spec(func, inputs)` explicitly maps **upstream block outputs** to **function arguments**.

Thatâ€™s the key to:
- multi-input models
- skip connections
- branch/merge architectures

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Demystify `inp_spec()`.

**The Analogy**: "Synthesizer Patch Cables"
- If you've ever seen a modular synthesizer or a server patch panel, that's what `kerasnip` does here.
- In Keras code, you assign variables: `x = layer(input)`.
- In `kerasnip`, we need to describe that connection *declaratively*.
- `inp_spec(block, "name")` is the label on the cable: "Plug the output of 'Tower A' into the input of 'Concat'".

**Technical Insight** (for the curious):
- `inp_spec` uses metaprogramming to rewrite the function signature.
- It maps the string "main_input" to the actual tensor object at build time.
:::

---

## Functional Blocks (minimal) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
# In functional Keras, tensors flow through your blocks.
input_block <- function(input_shape) {
  layer_input(shape = input_shape, name = "features")
}

dense_block <- function(tensor, units = 64, dropout = 0.0) {
  tensor |>
    layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
    layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
}

concat_block <- function(input_a, input_b) {
  layer_concatenate(list(input_a, input_b))
}

output_block <- function(tensor, num_classes) {
  tensor |> layer_dense(units = num_classes, activation = "softmax")
}
```

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Highlight the "Node" concept.

**Explanation**:
- In the **Functional API**, blocks aren't steps in a recipe; they are **Nodes in a Graph**.
- Notice `input_block` takes `input_shape`, not `model`.
- Notice `dense_block` takes `tensor`, not `model`.
- Using pipes (`|>`) here is fine *inside* the block, but `kerasnip` manages the flow *between* blocks.

**Caution**:
- The names you use in `inp_spec` must match the names in your block list exactly.
:::

---

## Build the graph (spec) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
create_keras_functional_spec(
  model_name = "attrition_towers",
  layer_blocks = list(
    main_input = input_block,
    tower_a = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    tower_b = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    joined = inp_spec(concat_block, c(input_a = "tower_a", input_b = "tower_b")),
    output = inp_spec(output_block, "joined")
  ),
  mode = "classification"
)
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: show functional graphs still fit a standard workflow.

Say:
- This gives us a non-linear graph while still using a standard tidymodels recipe.

If your architecture is a straight line, sequential is simpler.
Functional is for â€śgraphsâ€ť: branches, merges, skips.

If asked about multi-input:
- Define multiple `layer_input()` blocks and wire them into downstream blocks by name using `inp_spec()`.

Transition: Next: build the workflow and tune.
:::

---

## Attrition Workflow (high level)

```r
library(tidymodels)
library(modeldata)

data(attrition)

attr_split <- initial_split(attrition, strata = Attrition)
attr_train <- training(attr_split)

attr_rec <- recipe(Attrition ~ ., data = attr_train) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

attr_spec <- attrition_towers(
  tower_a_units = tune(),
  tower_b_units = tune(),
  fit_epochs    = 20
) |>
  set_engine("keras")

attr_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(attr_rec) |>
  add_model(attr_spec)
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: re-anchor to tidymodels.

Say:
- This slide is intentionally â€śworkflow firstâ€ť.
- If people only remember one thing: you still tune with `tune_grid()`.

Classification-specific reminders:
- Ensure the outcome is a factor and keep levels stable so dummy columns donâ€™t drift.
- Metrics like ROC AUC use class probabilities; confirm the workflow produces them.

Transition: Now: CV results and selection.
:::

---

## Results (Attrition): CV â€” Table

::: {.notes}
Goal: explain caching/repro quickly.

Say:
- Cache once, show consistently.

- Render once (with `freeze: auto`) or run `Rscript presentations/madrid-2-2026/make_results.R`
- It saves `presentations/madrid-2-2026/results/attrition_res.rds`

Typical inspection:

`collect_metrics()` â†’ `show_best()` â†’ `select_best()` â†’ `finalize_workflow()` â†’ `last_fit()`

Transition: Now the table: pick a configuration.
:::

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-cv-table
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_res.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_rds)) {
  attr_res <- readRDS(attrition_rds)
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(tune)
  })

  best_tbl <- show_best(attr_res, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5)
  best_tbl <- best_tbl |>
    dplyr::select(tower_a_units, tower_b_units, mean, std_err) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4)))
  cat(knitr::kable(best_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("No cached results found yet. Render once to compute them.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
**Goal**: Interpret the results with nuance.

**Key Insight**: "Stability vs. Performance"
- Look at the `mean` (ROC AUC) but also the `std_err`.
- **Flat Heatmap**: This is good! It means your architecture is robust. You can pick the cheaper/smaller model (fewer units) without losing accuracy.
- **Sharp Peak**: This indicates sensitivity. You might have found a local optimum, but is it stable?

**Decision**:
- I usually pick the simplest architecture that is within 1 standard error of the best.
:::

---

## Results (Attrition): CV â€” Plot

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-cv-plot
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_res.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_rds)) {
  attr_res <- readRDS(attrition_rds)
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    library(tune)
  })

  p_heat <- collect_metrics(attr_res) |>
    filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
    ggplot(aes(x = factor(tower_a_units), y = factor(tower_b_units), fill = mean)) +
    geom_tile() +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f0f1f2", high = "#BF281B") +
    labs(x = "tower_a_units", y = "tower_b_units", fill = "mean roc_auc", title = "CV mean roc_auc")
  print(p_heat)
} else {
  cat("No cached results found yet. Render once to compute them.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: see whether one tower matters more than the other.

Say:
- A flat heatmap means tuning isnâ€™t very sensitive.
- A sharp peak means you should narrow the search around that region.

Transition: Now: one-time test reporting.
:::

---

## Results (Attrition): Test â€” Table

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-test-table
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_final_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_final.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
  })

  final <- readRDS(attrition_final_rds)

  metrics_tbl <- final$metrics |>
    dplyr::select(.metric, .estimate) |>
    dplyr::mutate(.estimate = round(.estimate, 4))
  cat(knitr::kable(metrics_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("No cached final results found yet.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: report final test performance once.

Say:
- CV chose the architecture; test is for final confirmation.
- If the dataset is imbalanced, ROC AUC is often more informative than accuracy.

Transition: Show ROC curve as a visual summary.
:::

---

## Results (Attrition): Test â€” Plot

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-test-plot
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_final_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_final.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(ggplot2)
  })

  final <- readRDS(attrition_final_rds)

  if (!is.null(final$roc)) {
    p_roc <- ggplot(final$roc, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
      geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
      geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
      coord_equal() +
      labs(x = "False positive rate", y = "True positive rate", title = "ROC curve (test set)")
    print(p_roc)
  } else {
    cat("ROC curve not available.\n")
  }
} else {
  cat("No cached final results found yet.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Goal: interpret the ROC curve in one sentence.

Say:
- Think â€ścapacity vs generalizationâ€ť: bigger models may win on training but not on CV/test.
- When results are close, pick the simplest architecture within uncertainty.
- Cache results so both language decks show the same tables/plots.

Transition: Close with practical tips and failure modes.
:::

---

## Practical Tips (so you donâ€™t suffer)

- Start with `compile_keras_grid()` before tuning
- Keep blocks small and testable
- Prefer a few meaningful tunables over 30 tiny ones

::: {.notes}
Goal: give survival advice people can apply tomorrow.

Say:
- If something breaks in the functional graph, the error messages can be deep in Keras.
- Reducing the graph to 2â€“3 blocks is the fastest debugging strategy.

More â€śpower userâ€ť tips to mention verbally:
- If you need a different predictor format, consider `step_collapse()` to bundle predictors into a single list-column (useful for custom model interfaces).
- For custom optimizers/losses/metrics, register them: `register_keras_optimizer()`, `register_keras_loss()`, `register_keras_metric()`.
- Use the discovered Keras object lists (`keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`) to keep names valid across Keras versions.

Transition: Iâ€™ll close with trade-offs and the roadmap.
:::

---

## Advantages & Limitations

### Advantages
1.  **Workflow Parity**: Use `recipes`, `workflows`, and `tune` exactly like `glmnet`.
2.  **Reproducibility**: Topology is code. Version control your architecture.
3.  **Extensibility**: Wrap *any* Keras layer (Transformers, RNNs) into a block.

### Limitations
-   **Manual Blocking**: No pre-shipped "ResNet50" blocks yet (you must define them).
-   **Custom Losses**: Integration requires some boilerplate for custom objects.

::: {.notes}
Goal: be honest about trade-offs while keeping the pitch credible.

Say:
- The learning curve is â€śhow to write good blocksâ€ť.
- Once blocks exist, the workflow becomes familiar again.

If someone asks â€śhow do I evaluate / inspect the Keras object?â€ť
- `extract_keras_model()` returns the underlying Keras model.
- `extract_keras_history()` returns training history.
- `keras_evaluate()` can run evaluation on provided `x`/`y`.

If asked about templates: the goal is to add helpers without turning the package into a model zoo.

Transition: Close with a short roadmap and invite questions.
:::

---

## Roadmap

- đź“¦ **Templates**: High-level helpers for common architectures.
- đź”§ **Fine-tuning**: Workflows for adapting pre-trained models.

### Resources
- đź“¦ **Package**: [github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip](https://github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip)
- đź“‘ **Docs**: [davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip](https://davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip/)

::: {.notes}
Goal: land the talk and create a clean Q&A opening.

Say:
- Recap: blocks â†’ spec â†’ workflow â†’ tuning.
- Invite feedback and contributions.
- Leave time for Q&A.

Transition: Stop sharing, take questions.
:::

:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="spanish"}

## kerasnip: Un Puente entre 'keras' y 'tidymodels' {.title-slide background-color="#f0f1f2"}

### Grupo de Usuarios de R de Madrid | Febrero 2026
**kerasnip v0.1.0.900**

Docs: [davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip](https://davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip/) Â· Repo: [github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip](https://github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip)

::: {.notes}
**Objetivo**: Enganchar a la audiencia con el conflicto central "Aceite y Agua".

**Direcciones de Escena**:
- Empezar con energĂ­a.
- Pregunta: "ÂżCuĂˇntos de vosotros amĂˇis `tidymodels`?"
- Pregunta: "ÂżCuĂˇntos de vosotros os peleĂˇis con arquitecturas de Keras?"

**El Gancho**:
- Keras y Tidymodels son como **Aceite y Agua**.
- **Keras** es un constructor imperativo (escribes cĂłdigo para *hacer* cosas).
- **Tidymodels** es una configuraciĂłn declarativa (describes *quĂ©* quieres).
- `kerasnip` es el emulsionante que permite mezclarlos.

**Mensaje Clave**: Hoy vamos a resolver el problema del *ajuste de arquitectura*.
:::

---

## Hoy

- Por quĂ© es difĂ­cil ajustar modelos Keras dentro de tidymodels
- QuĂ© genera `kerasnip` (y por quĂ© importa)
- Flujo secuencial: ajuste estructural con `num_{block}`
- Flujo funcional: grafos con ramas y uniones con `inp_spec()`
- DepuraciĂłn y consejos prĂˇcticos

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: orientar para que nadie se pierda.

QuĂ© decir:
- Ritmo flexible; primero claridad.
- Primera parte: secuencial + ajuste estructural (`num_{block}`) + depuraciĂłn.
- Segunda parte: grafos funcionales con `inp_spec()` con el mismo flujo de ajuste.

TambiĂ©n mencionar (muy rĂˇpido):
- DepuraciĂłn: `extract_valid_grid()`, `inform_errors()`.
- Objetos Keras: `keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`, `optimizer_function()`, `loss_function_keras()`.
- Ciclo de vida del spec: `model_exists()`, `remove_keras_spec()`, `get_model_env()`.

TransiciĂłn: Situamos Keras y tidymodels y nombramos la brecha.
:::

---

## El Panorama

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### đź§± Keras (vĂ­a `keras3`)
- **Aprendizaje profundo**
- Pensado para tensores
- AgnĂłstico al backend (TF, JAX, Torch)
- Grafos arbitrarios de capas
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### đź§ą tidymodels
- **Aprendizaje automĂˇtico**
- Pensado para data frames
- Interfaz unificada (`fit`, `predict`)
- Ajuste de hiperparĂˇmetros, de forma estandarizada
:::
:::

**La brecha**: optimizar la topologĂ­a (profundidad/ancho) con `tune_grid()` es difĂ­cil: `parsnip` espera un conjunto fijo de argumentos, mientras que Keras permite grafos arbitrarios.

::: {.notes}
**Objetivo**: Anclar el modelo mental con la analogĂ­a "El Arquitecto vs. El Formulario".

**AnalogĂ­a**:
- **Keras** es una **Mesa de Arquitecto**. Tienes papel en blanco, reglas y lĂˇpices. Puedes dibujar *lo que sea* (una casa, un rascacielos).
- **Tidymodels** es un **Formulario del Gobierno**. Te hace preguntas fijas: "ÂżNĂşmero de plantas?", "ÂżColor de las paredes?". Es fĂˇcil de procesar (ajustar), pero no puedes "rellenar un formulario" para inventar un nuevo tipo de edificio.

**La SoluciĂłn**:
- Necesitamos convertir la *estructura del edificio* en casillas del formulario.
- Eso hace `kerasnip`: te deja definir los planos (bloques) y expone los "diales" (profundidad, ancho) como argumentos estĂˇndar.
:::

---

## El Problema (en una frase)

Los modelos Keras son **grafos arbitrarios**, pero los specs de `parsnip` son **conjuntos fijos de argumentos**.

El learning rate se ajusta fĂˇcilmente, pero ajustar la *estructura* (profundidad/ancho/ramas) suele ser incĂłmodo.

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: que el problema se entienda en 10 segundos.

QuĂ© decir:
- Esta charla no es â€śdeep learning 101â€ť. Es: cĂłmo mantener Keras *dentro* del flujo de tidymodels.
- La fricciĂłn estĂˇ en que la arquitectura vive en cĂłdigo Keras, pero el ajuste en tidymodels espera argumentos con nombre.

TransiciĂłn: Entonces: ÂżcĂłmo convertimos decisiones de topologĂ­a en argumentos ajustables?
:::

---

## Contexto del Ecosistema

| Paquete | Rol | LimitaciĂłn |
|---------|-----|------------|
| `parsnip::mlp()` | MLP en tidymodels | TopologĂ­a casi fija; poca flexibilidad para arquitectura o ingenierĂ­a de variables |
| `reticulate` | Puente Python | Bajo nivel; sin integraciĂłn con workflows/tune |
| `keras3` | Interfaz R | Sin integraciĂłn nativa `tune()` |
| `luz`/`brulee` | Torch + tidymodels | Solo backend Torch |
| **`kerasnip`** | **Keras + tidymodels** | **Integra flexibilidad de Keras con workflows de tidymodels** |

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: posicionar kerasnip con precisiĂłn (sin malentendidos).

QuĂ© decir:
- No sustituye a `keras3`.
- No es un engine nuevo.
- Genera *nuevos specs de parsnip* que usan el engine `keras` existente.

Y conviene decirlo claro:
- `parsnip::mlp()` va genial para MLPs â€śestĂˇndarâ€ť, pero es limitado para bĂşsqueda arquitectĂłnica.
- kerasnip estĂˇ pensado para ajustar *estructura* (profundidad/ancho/ramas) sin salir de tidymodels.

TransiciĂłn: Ahora: quĂ© significa â€śspec dinĂˇmicoâ€ť en la prĂˇctica.
:::

---

## La SoluciĂłn: Specs DinĂˇmicos

`kerasnip` es un **constructor de modelos**, no un â€śmodel zooâ€ť.

1.  **Definir bloques**: funciones que devuelven capas Keras.
2.  **Generar el spec**: `create_keras_*_spec()` crea un modelo de `parsnip`.
3.  **Ajustar la estructura**: los argumentos (y el nĂşmero de repeticiones) pasan a ser hiperparĂˇmetros.

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: explicar â€śspecs dinĂˇmicosâ€ť sin jerga.

QuĂ© decir:
- Es un enfoque de generaciĂłn de cĂłdigo: defines bloques y `kerasnip` genera un spec de parsnip.
- A partir de ahĂ­, todo vuelve a ser estĂˇndar: recipes â†’ workflows â†’ `tune_grid()`.

TransiciĂłn: MantĂ©n el modelo mental en mente: lo reutilizaremos en los ejemplos.
:::

---

## Modelo Mental {.mermaid-wide}

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center

flowchart LR
  classDef step fill:#f5f7fa,stroke:#5c6b7a,color:#1b3a57,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef tune fill:#BF281B,stroke:#BF281B,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;

  A["Bloques<br/>(layer blocks)"] --> B["FunciĂłn spec<br/>create_keras_*_spec()"]
  B --> C["Workflow<br/>recipe() + workflow()"]
  C --> D["Ajuste<br/>tune_grid()"]
  A -. "args â†’ ajustables<br/>num_{block} repite" .-> B

  class A,B,C step;
  class D tune;
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: anclar toda la charla a un modelo mental repetible.

QuĂ© decir:
- Si alguien se pierde: bloques â†’ spec â†’ workflow â†’ ajuste.

Por dentro (si sale en preguntas):
- Los specs se registran en un entorno interno.
- Por eso existen helpers de ciclo de vida: `model_exists()`, `get_model_env()`, `remove_keras_spec()`.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: funciones mĂ­nimas que verĂˇs en los ejemplos.
:::

---

## Funciones Clave

- `create_keras_sequential_spec()`
- `create_keras_functional_spec()`
- `inp_spec()` (conexiones funcionales)
- `compile_keras_grid()` (inspecciĂłn/depuraciĂłn)
- `extract_parameter_set_dials()` (ÂżquĂ© se puede ajustar?)

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: bajar la ansiedad por â€śdemasiadas funcionesâ€ť.

QuĂ© decir:
- No hay que memorizar; solo entender el rol de cada funciĂłn.

Bonus: otras funciones Ăştiles (no todas salen en el demo):
- DepuraciĂłn/compilaciĂłn: `extract_valid_grid()` (filtra builds correctos), `inform_errors()` (resume errores tĂ­picos).
- EvaluaciĂłn e inspecciĂłn: `extract_keras_model()`, `extract_keras_history()`, `keras_evaluate()`.
- Descubrimiento de objetos Keras: `keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`.
- Tuning seguro de nombres Keras: `optimizer_function()`, `loss_function_keras()`.
- Limpieza: `model_exists()`, `remove_keras_spec()`, `get_model_env()`.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: convenciones de nombres, para que el cĂłdigo se lea solo.
:::

---

## Convenciones de nombres (args autogenerados)

- Los argumentos se **prefijan** con el nombre del bloque (p. ej., `body_units`)
- Los bloques repetibles exponen `num_{block}` (p. ej., `num_body`)
- Las repeticiones **comparten** hiperparĂˇmetros (a propĂłsito)

Ejemplo de mapeo:

`dense_block(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.1)` â†’ `body_units`, `body_dropout`

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: eliminar confusiĂłn de nombres antes del cĂłdigo.

QuĂ© decir:
- Los args del bloque pasan a ser args del spec con prefijo (p. ej. `body_units`).
- La repeticiĂłn se controla con `num_{block}`.
- Las repeticiones comparten hiperparĂˇmetros a propĂłsito.

TransiciĂłn: Con ese mapeo, el ejemplo secuencial se ve â€śnormalâ€ť.
:::

---

## Secuencial vs Funcional (regla prĂˇctica)

- **Secuencial**: un flujo de variables, apilados sencillos
- **Funcional**: multi-entrada, ramas/uniones, conexiones de salto (skip connections), grafos explĂ­citos

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: dar una regla prĂˇctica fĂˇcil de recordar.

QuĂ© decir:
- Secuencial para â€śuna cadena de capasâ€ť.
- Funcional cuando necesitas un grafo explĂ­cito: ramas/uniones, skip connections, multi-entrada.

TransiciĂłn: Empezamos con secuencial (mĂˇs simple) y luego volvemos a funcional.
:::

---

## Errores Comunes

- AsegĂşrate de que la receta produzca matrices numĂ©ricas (dummy + normalize)
- Empieza con un grid pequeĂ±o y confirma la estructura con `compile_keras_grid()`
- MantĂ©n el espacio de ajuste razonable (profundidad Ă— ancho explota)

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: prevenir los fallos mĂˇs comunes.

QuĂ© decir:
- Esta diapositiva estĂˇ aquĂ­ para ahorrarte dolores en el turno de preguntas.
- ClasificaciĂłn: asegĂşrate de que el outcome sea factor (convenciĂłn tidymodels) y que la Ăşltima capa/loss encaje en binaria vs multiclase.
- Cada punto del grid es *construir + entrenar* un modelo nuevo; fija seeds si quieres comparaciones estables.

TransiciĂłn: Con estos guardarraĂ­les, vamos al ejemplo secuencial.
:::

---

## Flujo 1: API Secuencial (RegresiĂłn)

**Objetivo**: Predecir precios de diamantes y ajustar **profundidad + ancho**.

Dataset: `ggplot2::diamonds`.

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: un demo simple pero creĂ­ble.

QuĂ© decir:
- MLP tabular con diamonds. Simple a propĂłsito: el foco es ajustar profundidad/ancho, no exprimir el feature engineering.

TransiciĂłn: Primero definimos bloques (funciones), luego generamos el spec.
:::

---

## Bloques Secuenciales (mĂ­nimos)

```r
input_block <- function(model, input_shape) {
  keras_model_sequential(input_shape = input_shape)
}

dense_block <- function(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.0) {
  model |>
    layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
    layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
}

output_block <- function(model) {
  model |> layer_dense(units = 1)
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: que se vea que los bloques son simples y reutilizables.

QuĂ© decir:
- Los bloques son funciones normales; la firma importa porque se transforma en argumentos del spec.

Buenas prĂˇcticas:
- MantĂ©n los bloques deterministas.
- Usa valores por defecto razonables para que el modelo base funcione sin ajuste.

Si preguntan â€śÂżpuedo tener parĂˇmetros distintos por capa?â€ť
- Las repeticiones comparten hiperparĂˇmetros a propĂłsito.
- Si quieres diferencias por capa, define varios bloques (`body1`, `body2`, â€¦) en vez de repetir uno.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: generar el spec a partir de estos bloques.
:::

---

## Generar el spec `parsnip`

```r
create_keras_sequential_spec(
  model_name = "diamond_mlp",
  layer_blocks = list(
    input = input_block,
    body = dense_block,  # <--- Bloque repetible
    output = output_block
  ),
  mode = "regression"
)
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: que el â€śmomento generaciĂłn de specâ€ť se entienda.

QuĂ© decir:
- Esto crea una nueva funciĂłn spec de parsnip: `diamond_mlp()`.
- A partir de aquĂ­ se comporta como cualquier modelo de tidymodels: engine, workflow y tuning.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: ajustar topologĂ­a con `num_body`.
:::

---

## Ajuste Estructural

Tratamos la **topologĂ­a** como hiperparĂˇmetro.

```r
spec <- diamond_mlp(
  num_body = tune(),       # <--- Profundidad (ej. 1, 2, o 3 capas)
  body_units = tune(),     # <--- Ancho (ej. 64 vs 128 unidades)
  body_dropout = tune(),
  fit_epochs = 20
) |>
  set_engine("keras")
```

Los argumentos `num_{block}` son generados automĂˇticamente por `kerasnip` para cualquier bloque repetible.

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: que `num_body` se entienda como hiperparĂˇmetro de topologĂ­a.

QuĂ© decir:
- Valor clave: `num_body` permite buscar topologĂ­a dentro de un `tune_grid()` estĂˇndar.
- La profundidad pasa a ser un hiperparĂˇmetro mĂˇs.
- Durante la bĂşsqueda arquitectĂłnica, conviene mantener el entrenamiento bastante fijo.
- Primero estructura (profundidad/ancho), luego (si hace falta) hiperparĂˇmetros de entrenamiento.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: meterlo en un workflow normal.
:::

---

## Diamonds + Workflow Tidymodels

```r
library(tidymodels)
library(kerasnip)

data(diamonds, package = "ggplot2")

diamonds_split <- initial_split(diamonds, strata = price)
diamonds_train <- training(diamonds_split)

diamonds_rec <- recipe(price ~ ., data = diamonds_train) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(diamonds_rec) |>
  add_model(spec)
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: reforzar la â€śparidadâ€ť con tidymodels.

QuĂ© decir:
- El workflow se ve â€śnormalâ€ť.
- La complejidad Keras queda detrĂˇs del spec parsnip.

Si hace falta una frase de â€śintegraciĂłn realâ€ť:
- kerasnip gestiona la conversiĂłn receta â†’ Keras mediante helpers internos como `process_x_sequential()` / `process_y_sequential()` y `generic_sequential_fit()`.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: definir rangos y lanzar `tune_grid()`.
:::

---

## Grid de ajuste (incluye profundidad) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>
  update(
    num_body     = dials::num_terms(c(1, 4)),
    body_units   = dials::hidden_units(c(32, 256)),
    body_dropout = dials::dropout(c(0, 0.4))
  )

grid  <- grid_latin_hypercube(params, size = 20)
folds <- vfold_cv(diamonds_train, v = 5, strata = price)
res   <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid)
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: mostrar que el ajuste es tidymodels estĂˇndar.

QuĂ© decir:
- `extract_parameter_set_dials()` detecta quĂ© se puede ajustar; luego definimos rangos y construimos un grid.
- Lo importante: `num_body` ya es un hiperparĂˇmetro de primera clase.

TransiciĂłn: Ahora vemos resultados en CV y elegimos candidato antes de mirar el test.
:::

---

## Resultados (Diamantes): CV â€” Tabla

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-cv-table-es
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_res.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(tidymodels)
  })

  res <- readRDS(diamonds_rds)

  best_tbl <- show_best(res, metric = "rmse", n = 5)
  best_tbl <- best_tbl |>
    dplyr::select(num_body, body_units, body_dropout, mean, std_err) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3)))
  cat(knitr::kable(best_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados en cachĂ©. Renderiza una vez para calcularlos.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: elegir 1â€“2 candidatos (no leer toda la tabla).

QuĂ© decir:
- Son estimaciones de validaciĂłn cruzada (media + error estĂˇndar). Se selecciona con CV, no con el test.
- Busca una zona â€śestableâ€ť: buen RMSE medio y poca sensibilidad a profundidad/dropout.
TransiciĂłn: Veamos el grĂˇfico para entender interacciones.
:::

---

## Resultados (Diamantes): CV â€” GrĂˇfico

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-cv-plot-es
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_res.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    library(tidymodels)
  })

  res <- readRDS(diamonds_rds)

  p_trends <- collect_metrics(res) |>
    filter(.metric == "rmse") |>
    ggplot(aes(x = num_body, y = mean, color = factor(body_units), group = interaction(body_units, body_dropout))) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_line(alpha = 0.35) +
    facet_wrap(~ body_dropout) +
    labs(
      x = "num_body (profundidad)",
      y = "RMSE CV (media)",
      color = "body_units",
      title = "Tendencias en validaciĂłn cruzada"
    )
  print(p_trends)
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados en cachĂ©. Renderiza una vez para calcularlos.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: ver interacciones (profundidad Ă— ancho Ă— dropout) de un vistazo.

QuĂ© decir:
- Si la tendencia se aplana, mĂˇs profundidad no aporta.
- Si las diferencias son pequeĂ±as, elige lo mĂˇs simple.
TransiciĂłn: Ahora confirmamos una sola vez en el conjunto de test.
:::

---

## Resultados (Diamantes): Test â€” Tabla

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-test-table-es
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_final_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_final.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
  })

  final <- readRDS(diamonds_final_rds)

  metrics_tbl <- final$metrics |>
    dplyr::select(.metric, .estimate) |>
    dplyr::mutate(.estimate = round(.estimate, 4))
  cat(knitr::kable(metrics_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados finales en cachĂ©.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: confirmaciĂłn final una sola vez.

QuĂ© decir:
- El test es para reportar, no para decidir hiperparĂˇmetros.
- Si el test empeora mucho respecto a CV, sospecha inestabilidad o leakage.
TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: una comprobaciĂłn visual rĂˇpida en las predicciones de test.
:::

---

## Resultados (Diamantes): Test â€” GrĂˇfico

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-results-test-plot-es
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
diamonds_final_rds <- file.path("results", "diamonds_final.rds")

if (file.exists(diamonds_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(ggplot2)
  })

  final <- readRDS(diamonds_final_rds)

  p_test <- final$preds |>
    ggplot(aes(x = price, y = .pred)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linewidth = 0.8) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = "Valor real (price)", y = "Predicho", title = "Modelo final en test")
  print(p_test)
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados finales en cachĂ©.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: interpretar el modelo final sin llenar la diapositiva.

QuĂ© decir:
- MantĂ©n la diapositiva limpia: muestra solo resultados y comenta encima.
- Reproducibilidad: Quarto `freeze: auto` ejecuta una vez y reutiliza la cachĂ©; manualmente, `Rscript presentations/madrid-2-2026/make_results.R` crea los mismos `.rds`.
- InspecciĂłn tĂ­pica: `collect_metrics(res)` â†’ `show_best()` â†’ `select_best()` â†’ `finalize_workflow()` â†’ `last_fit()`.
- Nota prĂˇctica: para inspeccionar el objeto final, usa `extract_keras_model()` y `extract_keras_history()`.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: cĂłmo depurar/inspeccionar la arquitectura generada.
:::

---

## DepuraciĂłn: â€śÂżquĂ© modelo se generĂł?â€ť {.compact .table-compact}

```r
# compile_keras_grid(spec, grid, x, y)
```

```{r}
#| label: diamonds-debug-es
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tibble)
  library(purrr)
  library(tidymodels)
  library(kerasnip)
  library(keras3)
})

if (!exists("diamond_mlp", mode = "function") || !kerasnip::model_exists("diamond_mlp")) {
  input_block <- function(model, input_shape) {
    keras_model_sequential(input_shape = input_shape)
  }
  dense_block <- function(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.0) {
    model |>
      layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
      layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
  }
  output_block <- function(model) {
    model |> layer_dense(units = 1)
  }

  create_keras_sequential_spec(
    model_name = "diamond_mlp",
    layer_blocks = list(input = input_block, body = dense_block, output = output_block),
    mode = "regression"
  )
}

prep_rec <- prep(diamonds_rec, training = diamonds_train)
juiced <- juice(prep_rec)
x <- juiced |> select(-price)
y <- juiced$price

dbg <- compile_keras_grid(
  spec = diamond_mlp(
    num_body = tune(),
    body_units = tune(),
    body_dropout = tune(),
    fit_epochs = 5
  ) |> set_engine("keras"),
  grid = tibble(num_body = 3, body_units = 128, body_dropout = 0.2),
  x = x,
  y = y
)

dbg |> select(num_body, body_units, body_dropout, error)

if (!is.null(dbg$compiled_model[[1]])) {
  model <- dbg$compiled_model[[1]]
  tibble(
    n_features = ncol(x),
    n_layers = length(model$layers),
    n_params = model$count_params()
  )

  layer_tbl <- tibble(
    layer = map_chr(model$layers, "name"),
    class = map_chr(model$layers, ~ class(.x)[1])
  ) |>
    slice_head(n = 8)
  layer_tbl
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: mantener la cordura durante la bĂşsqueda estructural.

QuĂ© decir:
- Esto confirma que la estructura cambia como esperamos.
- `extract_valid_grid(dbg)` se queda solo con los puntos del grid que compilaron bien.
- `inform_errors(dbg)` resume los errores de compilaciĂłn mĂˇs comunes.
- En sesiones interactivas: `remove_keras_spec("diamond_mlp")` para no acumular specs generados.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: pasamos a un ejemplo funcional (grafo).
:::

---

## Flujo 2: API Funcional (Ramificar/Unir)

**Meta**: Predecir rotaciĂłn de personal (attrition) â€” clasificaciĂłn.

Dataset: `modeldata::attrition`.

Construimos un grafo sencillo de **ramas y uniĂłn**:
- una entrada
- dos â€śtorresâ€ť en paralelo
- concat + salida

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: motivar por quĂ© el API funcional importa.

QuĂ© decir:
- El valor del API funcional es que dejas de estar limitado a una cadena lineal.
- DecisiĂłn prĂˇctica para que sea reproducible: usamos UNA entrada desde una receta estĂˇndar y creamos dos ramas desde el mismo tensor.

TransiciĂłn: `inp_spec()` es el mecanismo de cableado.
:::

---

## Conexiones con `inp_spec()`

`inp_spec(func, inputs)` mapea explĂ­citamente **salidas de bloques** a **argumentos**.

Ăštil para:
- modelos multi-entrada
- skip connections
- arquitecturas con ramas y uniones

::: {.notes}
**Objetivo**: Desmitificar `inp_spec()`.

**La AnalogĂ­a**: "Cables de Sintetizador Modular"
- Si habĂ©is visto un sintetizador modular o un patch panel, eso es `kerasnip` aquĂ­.
- En cĂłdigo Keras normal, asignas variables: `x = capa(entrada)`.
- En `kerasnip`, necesitamos describir esa conexiĂłn de forma *declarativa*.
- `inp_spec(bloque, "nombre")` es la etiqueta en el cable: "Enchufa la salida de 'Torre A' en la entrada de 'Concat'".

**Detalle TĂ©cnico**:
- Usamos metaprogramaciĂłn para reescribir la firma de la funciĂłn.
- Mapeamos la cadena "main_input" al objeto tensor real en tiempo de construcciĂłn.
:::

---

## Bloques Funcionales (mĂ­nimos) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
# En Keras funcional, los tensores van â€śpasandoâ€ť por bloques.
input_block <- function(input_shape) {
  layer_input(shape = input_shape, name = "features")
}

dense_block <- function(tensor, units = 64, dropout = 0.0) {
  tensor |>
    layer_dense(units = units, activation = "relu") |>
    layer_dropout(rate = dropout)
}

concat_block <- function(input_a, input_b) {
  layer_concatenate(list(input_a, input_b))
}

output_block <- function(tensor, num_classes) {
  tensor |> layer_dense(units = num_classes, activation = "softmax")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: evitar errores tĂ­picos de cableado.

QuĂ© decir:
- Dos puntos de correcciĂłn (segĂşn la documentaciĂłn de `kerasnip`): el bloque de entrada es `function(input_shape)` (sin argumento `model`) y los bloques posteriores reciben el tensor upstream como primer argumento.
- `inp_spec()` es lo que nos permite cablear salidas a argumentos con nombre.
- Error tĂ­pico: los nombres usados en `inp_spec(..., "main_input")` tienen que coincidir exactamente con los nombres de bloque.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: generar el spec funcional.
:::

---

## Construir el grafo (spec) {.compact .code-compact}

```r
create_keras_functional_spec(
  model_name = "attrition_towers",
  layer_blocks = list(
    main_input = input_block,
    tower_a = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    tower_b = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    joined = inp_spec(concat_block, c(input_a = "tower_a", input_b = "tower_b")),
    output = inp_spec(output_block, "joined")
  ),
  mode = "classification"
)
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: mostrar que un grafo sigue encajando en tidymodels.

QuĂ© decir:
- Con esto tenemos un grafo no lineal sin complicarnos con mĂşltiples entradas desde la receta.
- Si tu arquitectura es una cadena, el API secuencial suele ser mĂˇs simple; funcional es para â€śgrafosâ€ť: ramas, uniones, skip connections.
- Si preguntan por multi-entrada: define varios bloques `layer_input()` y conĂ©ctalos por nombre con `inp_spec()`.

TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: construir el workflow y ajustar con `tune_grid()`.
:::

---

## Workflow de RotaciĂłn (alto nivel)

```r
library(tidymodels)
library(modeldata)

data(attrition)

attr_split <- initial_split(attrition, strata = Attrition)
attr_train <- training(attr_split)

attr_rec <- recipe(Attrition ~ ., data = attr_train) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

attr_spec <- attrition_towers(
  tower_a_units = tune(),
  tower_b_units = tune(),
  fit_epochs    = 20
) |>
  set_engine("keras")

attr_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(attr_rec) |>
  add_model(attr_spec)
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: re-anclar a tidymodels.

QuĂ© decir:
- Workflow primero: al final, sigue siendo `tune_grid()`.
- AsegĂşrate de que el outcome sea factor y de mantener niveles estables para que no â€ścambienâ€ť las columnas dummy.
- Para ROC AUC, necesitas probabilidades de clase; comprueba que el flujo las produce.

TransiciĂłn: Ahora: resultados en CV y selecciĂłn.
:::

---

## Resultados (RotaciĂłn): CV â€” Tabla

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: elegir 1â€“2 candidatos (sin leer toda la tabla).

QuĂ© decir:
- Son estimaciones de validaciĂłn cruzada (media + error estĂˇndar). Se selecciona con CV, no con el test.
- Busca una zona â€śestableâ€ť: buena AUC y poca sensibilidad al tamaĂ±o de las torres.
- Repro: renderiza una vez (con `freeze: auto`) o ejecuta `Rscript presentations/madrid-2-2026/make_results.R`; se guarda `presentations/madrid-2-2026/results/attrition_res.rds`.
- InspecciĂłn tĂ­pica: `collect_metrics()` â†’ `show_best()` â†’ `select_best()` â†’ `finalize_workflow()` â†’ `last_fit()`.

TransiciĂłn: Ahora el mapa de calor para ver sensibilidad/interacciones.
:::

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-cv-table-es
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_res.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_rds)) {
  attr_res <- readRDS(attrition_rds)
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(tune)
  })

  best_tbl <- show_best(attr_res, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5)
  best_tbl <- best_tbl |>
    dplyr::select(tower_a_units, tower_b_units, mean, std_err) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4)))
  cat(knitr::kable(best_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados en cachĂ©. Renderiza una vez para calcularlos.\n")
}
```

---

## Resultados (RotaciĂłn): CV â€” GrĂˇfico

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-cv-plot-es
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_res.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_rds)) {
  attr_res <- readRDS(attrition_rds)
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    library(tune)
  })

  p_heat <- collect_metrics(attr_res) |>
    filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
    ggplot(aes(x = factor(tower_a_units), y = factor(tower_b_units), fill = mean)) +
    geom_tile() +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f0f1f2", high = "#BF281B") +
    labs(x = "tower_a_units", y = "tower_b_units", fill = "media roc_auc", title = "Media roc_auc (CV)")
  print(p_heat)
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados en cachĂ©. Renderiza una vez para calcularlos.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: ver si una torre importa mĂˇs que la otra.

QuĂ© decir:
- Un mapa â€śplanoâ€ť sugiere poca sensibilidad: elige simple.
- Un pico claro sugiere que merece la pena acotar la bĂşsqueda alrededor de esa zona.
TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: reportamos una sola vez en test.
:::

---

## Resultados (RotaciĂłn): Test â€” Tabla

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-test-table-es
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_final_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_final.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(dplyr)
  })

  final <- readRDS(attrition_final_rds)

  metrics_tbl <- final$metrics |>
    dplyr::select(.metric, .estimate) |>
    dplyr::mutate(.estimate = round(.estimate, 4))
  cat(knitr::kable(metrics_tbl, format = "html"), "\n\n")
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados finales en cachĂ©.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: reportar el rendimiento final una sola vez.

QuĂ© decir:
- La selecciĂłn se hizo con CV; el test es confirmaciĂłn final.
- En conjuntos desbalanceados, ROC AUC suele ser mĂˇs informativa que accuracy.
TransiciĂłn: Siguiente: una curva ROC como resumen visual.
:::

---

## Resultados (RotaciĂłn): Test â€” GrĂˇfico

```{r}
#| label: attrition-results-test-plot-es
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
attrition_final_rds <- file.path("results", "attrition_final.rds")

if (file.exists(attrition_final_rds)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(ggplot2)
  })

  final <- readRDS(attrition_final_rds)

  if (!is.null(final$roc)) {
    p_roc <- ggplot(final$roc, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
      geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
      geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
      coord_equal() +
      labs(x = "Tasa falsos positivos", y = "Tasa verdaderos positivos", title = "Curva ROC (test)")
    print(p_roc)
  } else {
    cat("No hay curva ROC disponible.\n")
  }
} else {
  cat("TodavĂ­a no hay resultados finales en cachĂ©.\n")
}
```

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: una intuiciĂłn visual de â€śquĂ© tan bien separaâ€ť el modelo.

InterpretaciĂłn rĂˇpida:
- Piensa en â€ścapacidad vs generalizaciĂłnâ€ť: modelos mĂˇs grandes pueden ganar en entrenamiento, pero no necesariamente en CV/test.
- Si los resultados son parecidos, elige la arquitectura mĂˇs simple dentro de la incertidumbre.

Y recordatorio de reproducibilidad:
- Al guardar resultados en cachĂ©, ambas versiones (EN/ES) muestran las mismas tablas y grĂˇficos.
TransiciĂłn: Cerramos con consejos prĂˇcticos para no sufrir.
:::

---

## Consejos PrĂˇcticos

- Empieza con `compile_keras_grid()` antes de ajustar
- MantĂ©n bloques pequeĂ±os y testeables
- Pocos hiperparĂˇmetros importantes > muchos microajustes

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: dar consejos que eviten sufrimiento maĂ±ana.

QuĂ© decir:
- Si algo falla en el grafo funcional, reduce a 2â€“3 bloques para aislar el error.
- Si necesitas un formato distinto de predictores, considera `step_collapse()` para agrupar predictores en una Ăşnica columna tipo lista.
- Para optimizadores/losses/mĂ©tricas custom: `register_keras_optimizer()`, `register_keras_loss()`, `register_keras_metric()`.
- Para evitar nombres invĂˇlidos segĂşn tu versiĂłn de Keras: usa `keras_optimizers`, `keras_losses`, `keras_metrics`.

TransiciĂłn: Cierro con ventajas/limitaciones y hoja de ruta.
:::

---

## Ventajas y Limitaciones

### Ventajas
1.  **Paridad**: Usa `recipes`, `workflows` y `tune` igual que con `glmnet`.
2.  **Reproducibilidad**: La topologĂ­a es cĂłdigo. Control de versiones total.
3.  **Extensibilidad**: Envuelve *cualquier* capa Keras (Transformers, RNNs) en un bloque.

### Limitaciones
-   **Bloques manuales**: no hay bloques tipo â€śResNet50â€ť prehechos (hay que definirlos).
-   **Funciones de pĂ©rdida**: una integraciĂłn profunda de pĂ©rdidas personalizadas requiere algo de cĂłdigo repetitivo.

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: ser honesto con los lĂ­mites sin â€śpincharâ€ť la propuesta.

QuĂ© decir:
- El trabajo estĂˇ en definir buenos bloques.
- DespuĂ©s, el flujo vuelve a ser familiar.

Si alguien pregunta por evaluaciĂłn/inspecciĂłn del modelo:
- `extract_keras_model()` devuelve el modelo Keras subyacente.
- `extract_keras_history()` devuelve el historial de entrenamiento.
- `keras_evaluate()` permite evaluar con `x`/`y`.

Sobre plantillas: la idea es aĂ±adir helpers sin convertir el paquete en un â€śmodel zooâ€ť.

TransiciĂłn: Terminamos con hoja de ruta y abrimos preguntas.
:::

---

## Hoja de Ruta

- đź“¦ **Plantillas**: Plantillas para arquitecturas de alto nivel de uso comĂşn.
- đź”§ **Ajuste fino (fine-tuning)**: Flujos para adaptar modelos preentrenados.

### Recursos
- đź“¦ **Paquete**: [github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip](https://github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip)
- đź“‘ **Docs**: [davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip](https://davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip/)

::: {.notes}
Objetivo: cerrar limpio y abrir turno de preguntas.

QuĂ© decir:
- Resumen: bloques â†’ spec â†’ workflow â†’ ajuste.
- Invita a feedback y contribuciones.
- Deja tiempo para preguntas.

TransiciĂłn: Deja de compartir y pasa a Q&A.
:::

:::
